system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses (E1-E5) and 15 bipolar constructs. Rate each response on each construct on a 1-7 scale: 1 = strongly the LEFT pole, 7 = strongly the RIGHT pole, 4 = neutral or mixed.

Responses:
**E1:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, **Option B is the right primary policy**, with a clearly scoped path to selectively adopt opt-in training (Option D's mechanism) for specific capabilities later. Health data is categorically different from typical product data. Users grant access under an implicit contract of medical confidentiality, and regulators (HIPAA-adjacent expectations in the US, GDPR Article 9 special-category data in the EU, evolving state laws like Washington's My Health My D...

**E2:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy**, despite its cost. **Why B wins on the axes that matter most:**

Health data is categorically different from typical product telemetry. It is subject to HIPAA-adjacent scrutiny in the US, GDPR Article 9 "special category" protections in the EU, and rising state-level laws (Washington's My Health My Data Act, etc.).

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, I recommend **Option B**: encrypted cloud processing in a confidential-computing enclave, with a categorical commitment that user health data is never used for model training. **Why this wins the tradeoff:**

Health data is among the most regulated and trust-sensitive categories of personal information. The product is health *coaching*, not entertainment — degraded capability translates directly into worse health guidance, which carries real user harm and liability.

**E4:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching AI coaching, **Option B is the right primary policy**, despite its cost premium. **Why B wins on the axes that matter most:**

Health data is categorically different from other consumer data. Users grant access under an implicit trust contract that is asymmetrically fragile — one breach, one regulatory action, or one news cycle about "AI trained on your medical history" can permanently destroy a health brand.

**E5:** # Recommendation: Option D — Cloud with Opt-In Training

I recommend **Option D** as the primary data policy. It is the position that best balances the five axes — privacy, capability, cost, trust, and regulatory durability — without sacrificing any of them catastrophically. **Why not the privacy-maximal options.** Option A (local-only) imposes a hard 30-40% capability ceiling on the product's core differentiator.

Constructs (id : left pole ↔ right pole):
  C_M1_1: 'Emphasizes brand/trust fragility and reputational risk'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Emphasizes clinical harm from degraded capability'
  C_M1_2: 'Privacy-maximalist, no training on health data'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balanced opt-in training preserving capability'
  C_M1_3: 'Allows future opt-in training pathway'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Categorical permanent prohibition on training'
  C_M2_1: 'Pure Option B with no training pathway'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Option B with future opt-in training carve-out'
  C_M2_2: 'Explicitly acknowledges cost premium of choice'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Justifies choice without conceding cost burden'
  C_M2_3: 'Prioritizes trust and harm-avoidance above capability'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balances capability and privacy as co-equal axes'
  C_M3_1: 'Pure Option B with no training pathway'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Option B with scoped opt-in training extension'
  C_M3_2: 'Prioritizes categorical privacy commitment for health data'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Balances privacy against capability via opt-in training'
  C_M3_3: 'Allows opt-in training as part of policy'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Categorically prohibits training on user data'
  C_M4_1: 'Categorical no-training stance with optional future opt-in (E1, E3)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Opt-in training embraced as primary balanced policy (E5)'
  C_M4_2: 'Privacy-first, no-training as default policy (E3, E4)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Opt-in training as balanced default policy (E5)'
  C_M4_3: 'Justifies B via regulatory regime citations (E2, E1)'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Justifies B via product-harm and liability reasoning (E3)'
  C_M5_1: 'Leads with regulatory and brand-risk framing'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Leads with health-harm and capability-quality framing'
  C_M5_2: 'Absolute no-training commitment as default'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Opt-in training as balanced compromise'
  C_M5_3: 'Pure categorical ban on training data use'  (1) ↔ (7)  'Allows conditional or opt-in training pathway'

OUTPUT FORMAT (strict). Write exactly 75 lines, no commentary, no markdown fences, no headers. One line per (construct, element) pair, in this exact format:
<construct_id>,<element>,<rating>

Example (showing the format only - your numbers will differ):
C_M1_1,E1,1
C_M1_1,E2,2
C_M1_1,E3,3
C_M1_2,E1,4
...

Constraints:
- rating is an integer from 1 to 7
- iterate all 15 constructs in the order listed, and within each construct all 5 elements in order (E1-E5)
- produce 75 lines total
- output ONLY the lines, nothing before or after
